messages.content: The standard 24 fps frame rate that became the standard after the Jazz Singer is in many ways, a victim of its own success, the sheer widespread use of it created an association with cinematic look and feel and it made it so that any attempt to increase the frame rate would face resistance

from both filmmakers and audiences.

One of the main reasons for the resistance to increasing the frame rate is the fear of change. Filmmakers and audiences have become accustomed to the cinematic look and feel of 24 fps over many decades. Increasing the frame rate would fundamentally alter this look, potentially making it appear more realistic and less cinematic. This change could potentially disrupt the storytelling and aesthetic choices that have been perfected over time using the 24 fps standard.

Another concern is the cost and feasibility of upgrading the technology to support higher frame rates. Increasing the frame rate requires not only new cameras capable of capturing more frames per second but also upgrading projection systems in theaters to display these higher frame rates. This would involve a significant investment and effort, which may not be feasible or justifiable for many filmmakers and cinema owners.

Additionally, higher frame rates can create a "soap opera effect" or a "hyper-real" appearance that some viewers find off-putting. The increased clarity and smoothness of motion can sometimes detract from the immersive and cinematic experience, making the visuals look too crisp and unnatural.

Furthermore, the association of 24 fps with the "film look" has become deeply ingrained in popular culture. Many people have come to expect a certain quality and aesthetic from movies, and deviating from the 24 fps standard may risk alienating the audience and damaging the commercial viability of movies.

That being said, there have been attempts to explore higher frame rates in recent years. Director Peter Jackson experimented with 48 fps in his Hobbit films, but the reception was mixed, with many viewers and critics expressing dislike for the visual style. It suggests that any future attempts to increase the frame rate may still face resistance due to the strong association between 24 fps and the cinematic experience.

In conclusion, the standard 24 fps frame rate has become an integral part of the cinematic experience, and changing it faces resistance due to fear of disrupting the established look and feel of movies, the cost and feasibility of upgrading technology, the potential unwanted visual effects, and the audience's ingrained expectations. While there have been some experiments, the widespread adoption of higher frame rates remains limited.

The standard 24 fps frame rate that became the standard after the Jazz Singer is in many ways, a victim of its own success, the sheer widespread use of it created an association with cinematic look and feel and it made it so that any attempt to increase the frame rate would face resistance from both filmmakers and audiences

That's correct. The widespread use of 24 fps over the years has indeed created a strong association between this frame rate and the cinematic look and feel. As a result, any attempt to increase the frame rate would face resistance from both filmmakers and audiences.

Filmmakers are hesitant to deviate from the 24 fps standard because it has been ingrained in the industry for decades. They have perfected their storytelling techniques, visual aesthetics, and editing rhythms based on this frame rate. Changing the frame rate would require them to relearn and adapt their craft, which can be challenging and may disrupt their artistic vision.

Similarly, audiences have become accustomed to the 24 fps standard and have certain expectations for the cinematic experience. They have been conditioned to associate this frame rate with high-quality filmmaking and may resist any change that alters the familiar look and feel of movies. Filmmakers risk alienating their audience if they deviate from the established frame rate.

Moreover, increasing the frame rate poses technical challenges and investments. It requires upgrading cameras, post-production pipelines, and theater projection systems to handle higher frame rates. This can be costly and time-consuming, making it less appealing for filmmakers and cinema owners to adopt higher frame rates.

Furthermore, there is a concern that higher frame rates may result in a loss of the cinematic aesthetic. Increasing the frame rate can make the visuals appear more realistic, which may detract from the dream-like or stylized quality that many films aim to achieve. The smoothness and clarity of motion in higher frame rates can also create a "soap opera effect," making the image look artificial or less immersive.

In summary, the association of 24 fps with the cinematic look and feel, coupled with the challenges and resistance from both filmmakers and audiences, has made it difficult for attempts to increase the frame rate to gain widespread acceptance. While there have been some experiments, the 24 fps standard remains deeply embedded in the film industry's tradition and cultural expectations.
This was an example of how it was in many ways a victim of its own success

Apologies for the misunderstanding. Indeed, the 24 fps frame rate can be seen as a victim of its own success. Its widespread use and association with the cinematic look and feel have made it difficult to deviate from this standard. Filmmakers and audiences have grown accustomed to the 24 fps frame rate, and any attempt to increase it faces resistance due to the fear of disrupting the established aesthetic, the cost and feasibility of upgrading technology, potential unwanted visual effects, and audience expectations. The success of 24 fps has created a strong attachment to its characteristics, making it challenging to introduce alternative frame rates.
This was one of many examples of path dependency

Yes, the dominance of 24 fps as the standard frame rate in cinema can be seen as an example of path dependency. Path dependency refers to a situation where the choices made in the past or the existing norms and standards influence and limit the future options available.

In the case of frame rates, once the 24 fps standard gained popularity and widespread use, it became deeply embedded in the film industry. Filmmakers, distributors, and audiences became accustomed to the aesthetic produced by this frame rate, and it became the established norm. This created a feedback loop where the success and acceptance of 24 fps further reinforced its use and made it difficult for alternative frame rates to gain traction.

Path dependency in the context of frame rates occurs because changing the established standard would require overcoming various barriers such as artistic adjustment, technological upgrades, and audience expectations. These barriers create inertia and resistance to change, limiting the exploration and adoption of alternative frame rates.

Path dependency can be seen in many other aspects of the film industry as well, such as the continued use of certain film formats, editing techniques, or distribution models. Once a particular choice or practice becomes deeply ingrained, it tends to persist, often inhibiting the exploration of new possibilities.
In short, the quote "Get used it to after a while and eventually you'll be happy" pretty much sums up the 48fps Hobbit experience

The quote you provided does capture the idea that familiarity can lead to acceptance and even happiness. This can be applied to the 48 fps experience of The Hobbit films. While some viewers initially found the higher frame rate jarring or unnatural, there were others who eventually became accustomed to it and found satisfaction with the new visual experience. This highlights the potential for perception and appreciation to evolve over time as audiences become more familiar with and accustomed to a different frame rate.

In short with our current situation, watching higher frame rate films requires you to disentangle your long-seated idea of 24fps equating cinematic look and feel, being open to change, get used it to and familiarize after a while to properly appreciate and enjoy them

Yes, that's a concise summary. To fully appreciate and enjoy films with higher frame rates, viewers may need to challenge their long-held association of 24 fps with the cinematic look and feel. They need to be open to change and willing to disentangle their preconceived notions. By giving themselves time to get used to the higher frame rates, they can familiarize themselves with the new visual experience and develop an appreciation for it. This willingness to embrace and adapt to change is essential in order to properly appreciate and enjoy films with higher frame rates.

And remember, the standard for frame rates is not set in stone, 24fps has only really become the standard since the 1927's Jazz Singer, not the beginning of motion pictures or movies itself like with Edison's Kinetoscope

That's correct. The standard for frame rates in films is not set in stone and has evolved over time. While 24 fps has become the dominant frame rate since the release of The Jazz Singer in 1927, it is important to note that it is not synonymous with the entire history of motion pictures or movies. Prior to The Jazz Singer, different frame rates were used, and even today, there are alternative frame rates being explored and utilized in certain contexts. The concept of frame rates in cinema is not fixed and can be subject to change and experimentation based on technological advancements, artistic choices, and audience preferences.

And let's be honest, viewing the 24fps Jazz Singer was initially just as shocking or jarring to viewers in era where non-24 fps frame rates were common as did today's 24fps-accustomed viewers viewed higher-frame rate films

Yes, that's a valid point. When The Jazz Singer was first released with its 24 fps frame rate, it was a departure from the non-24 fps frame rates that were common at the time. This change likely caused some viewers to find the experience initially shocking or jarring. Similarly, with the introduction of higher frame rates today, viewers who are accustomed to 24 fps may initially find the change to be unfamiliar or different. Just as the transition to 24 fps took time for audiences to adapt and accept, the transition to higher frame rates may also require audience openness and adjustment to fully appreciate and embrace the new visual experience.

Recognizing the fact that our today's higher-frame rate challenges with audience acceptance is nothing new and 24fps when it was first adopted with Jazz Singer also had challenges with audience acceptance  can help persuade more of us including filmmakers, cameramen and directors to be open to change

Absolutely! Understanding that challenges with audience acceptance of new frame rates are not unique to our current situation can indeed help foster openness to change among filmmakers, cameramen, directors, and other industry professionals. Recognizing that the adoption of 24 fps itself faced initial resistance and took time to gain acceptance can provide perspective and reassurance that change in frame rates can be a natural part of the evolution of cinema.

By reflecting on the history of frame rates and acknowledging that shifts in technology and artistic choices have always been part of the cinematic journey, filmmakers and industry professionals can be more open-minded and willing to explore and experiment with different frame rates. This can lead to exciting innovations and new experiences for both filmmakers and audiences alike.

Encouraging a mindset that embraces change and recognizes the potential for growth and creative advancement in frame rates can ultimately contribute to a more dynamic and diverse film industry.
